{"query":"Voyager 1 RTG power depletion engineering data transmission end year estimate","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1903,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://science.nasa.gov/blogs/voyager/2026/04/17/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-keep-spacecraft-operating/","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Keep Spacecraft Operating - NASA Science","content":"Apr 17, 2026 — Voyager 1 on April 17, 2026. Both probes lose about 4 watts of power each year. Voyager 1's power levels fell unexpectedly.
Of the 10 identical sets of instruments that each spacecraft carries, seven have been shut off so far.
The team shut off the LECP on Voyager 2 in March 2025."},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voyager_1","title":"Voyager 1 - Wikipedia","content":"Its radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) may supply enough electric power to return engineering data until 2036.
Each MHW-RTG contains 24 pressed plutonium-238 PuO2 oxide spheres.
If the same failure had occurred during Voyager 2's launch a few weeks earlier, the Centaur would have run out of propellant before the probe reached the correct trajectory.
Voyager 1's initial orbit had an aphelion of 8.9 AU (830 million mi), just a little short of Saturn's orbit of 9.5 AU (880 million mi).
Scientists at the Johns Hopkins University Applied Physics Laboratory believe that Voyager 1 entered the termination shock in February 2003.
Using the TCM thrusters allowed Voyager 1 to continue transmitting data to NASA for two to three more years.
NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory published a statement on May 18, 2022, that the AACS was functional but sending invalid data.
On May 22, NASA announced that Voyager 1 "resumed returning science data from two of its four instruments", with work towards the others ongoing.
On June 13, NASA confirmed that the probe returns data from all four instruments.
"NASA's Voyager 1 spacecraft loses another instrument to save power"."},{"url":"https://spacedaily.com/d-the-most-distant-human-made-object-in-the-universe-is-voyager-1-currently-more-than-15-billion-miles-from-earth-and-its-plutonium-power-source-is-losing-about-four-watts-of-output-every-yea/","title":"The most distant human-made object in the universe is Voyager 1, currently more than 15 billion miles from Earth — and its plutonium power source is losing about four watts of output every year, meani","content":"The RTG produced about 470 watts at launch in 1977.
BBC Sky at Night Magazine notes that NASA estimates Voyager 1 will pass within 1.7 light-years of the faint red dwarf star Gliese 445 around the year 40,272.
The end of Voyager 1’s transmissions will mark the end of the longest continuous conversation any human civilization has ever conducted with anything beyond the immediate vicinity of its home planet.
It has involved thousands of scientists and engineers across multiple generations.
It will, sometime around 2030, simply end.
The engineering accomplishment of having kept the spacecraft operating for nearly fifty years, against the original five-year mission parameters, is not in any meaningful doubt regardless of what the Big Bang Project produces."},{"url":"https://satnews.com/2026/04/19/nasa-shuts-off-instrument-on-voyager-1-to-extend-interstellar-mission/","title":"NASA Shuts Off Instrument on Voyager 1 to Extend Interstellar Mission – SatNews","content":"On Friday, April 17, 2026, engineers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) sent commands to deactivate one of the few remaining science instruments aboard Voyager 1.
NASA plans to test the Big Bang procedure on Voyager 2 in May and June 2026, as the twin probe is closer to Earth and has slightly more power to spare.
On November 15, 2026, the spacecraft is projected to reach a symbolic distance of exactly one light-day from Earth—approximately 16.1 billion miles (25.9 billion km)."},{"url":"https://www.19fortyfive.com/2026/07/voyager-1-isnt-dying-because-anything-broke-after-almost-50-years-its-simply-running-out-of-power-and-nasa-is-shutting-off-its-instruments-one-by-one-until-the-signal-goes-silent/","title":"Voyager 1 isn't dying because anything broke — after almost 50 years it's simply running out of power, and NASA is shutting off its instruments one by one until the signal goes silent. - 19F","content":"Every year, Voyager’s plutonium-powered electrical system produces roughly four fewer watts, and now, NASA engineers are being forced to make irreversible decisions about which scientific instruments to shut down permanently.
Nearly fifty years later, Voyager is still transmitting scientific data back to Earth, despite being about 15 billion miles away.
Each remaining instrument represents one more difficult decision."}]}

The probe made flybys \"Flyby (spaceflight)\") of Jupiter, Saturn, and Saturn's largest moon, Titan \"Titan (moon)\"). As part of the Voyager program and like its sister craft *Voyager 2*, the spacecraft's extended mission is to locate and study the regions and boundaries of the outer heliosphere and to begin exploring the interstellar medium.
